Daily US-Israel-Iran War Brief: 
 9th May 2026  

 - We will be posting daily war updates on the ongoing war in Middle East   
  - ⁠Follow us for daily Brief. 
 
● The US-Iran ceasefire continues to hold as Qatar throws its support behind Pakistan-led mediation efforts aimed at reaching a lasting diplomatic agreement and reducing regional tensions. 

 ● The IDF dismantled an Islamic Jihad weapons production site and weapons storage facility in northern Gaza. The site was also used by Hamas for manufacturing explosive devices and storing weapons targeting Israeli civilians and IDF troops. 

 ● The Qatar-owned LNG carrier Al Kharaitiyat was approaching the Strait of Hormuz en route to Pakistan’s Port Qasim. 

 ● Bahrain’s Ministry of Interior announced the arrest of 41 individuals linked to the IRGC, describing it as a successful operation against foreign-backed security threats.  

● Trump warned that if negotiations with Iran fail to reach a conclusion, the US will resume and expand its naval escort mission, reinstating Project Freedom as “Project Freedom Plus”. 

 ● As of today, CENTCOM has redirected 58 commercial vessels and disabled 4 vessels since April 13 to prevent them from entering or leaving Iranian ports in violation of the naval blockade. 

 END OF THE BRIEF.

Daily US-Israel-Iran War Brief:
9th May 2026

- We will be posting daily war updates on the ongoing war in Middle East
- ⁠Follow us for daily Brief.

● The US-Iran ceasefire continues to hold as Qatar throws its support behind Pakistan-led mediation efforts aimed at reaching a lasting diplomatic agreement and reducing regional tensions.

● The IDF dismantled an Islamic Jihad weapons production site and weapons storage facility in northern Gaza. The site was also used by Hamas for manufacturing explosive devices and storing weapons targeting Israeli civilians and IDF troops.

● The Qatar-owned LNG carrier Al Kharaitiyat was approaching the Strait of Hormuz en route to Pakistan’s Port Qasim.

● Bahrain’s Ministry of Interior announced the arrest of 41 individuals linked to the IRGC, describing it as a successful operation against foreign-backed security threats.

● Trump warned that if negotiations with Iran fail to reach a conclusion, the US will resume and expand its naval escort mission, reinstating Project Freedom as “Project Freedom Plus”.

● As of today, CENTCOM has redirected 58 commercial vessels and disabled 4 vessels since April 13 to prevent them from entering or leaving Iranian ports in violation of the naval blockade.

END OF THE BRIEF.

Netram Defence Review (@NetramDefence)

- We will be posting daily war updates on the ongoing war in Middle East
- ⁠Follow us for daily Brief.

● Saudi Arabia and Kuwait restored full US access to their military bases and airspace following the launch of Project Freedom.

tweets.title:
Who is India's next CDS, Lt. Gen. NS Raja Subramani?

Commissioned into the Garhwal Rifles in December 1985, Lt Gen Subramani has a career spanning over 37 years in the Indian Army. 
Over the course of his service, he has served across a wide range of conflict zones and terrains. He has held several Command, Staff and Instructional appointments.

Lt Gen Subramani is a graduate of the National Defence Academy (NDA) and the Indian Military Academy (IMA).
He has also attended the Joint Services Command and Staff College in Bracknell, United Kingdom, and the National Defence College in New Delhi.

He also holds a Master of Arts degree from King's College London and an MPhil in Defence Studies from Madras University. 

He has been awarded some of India’s highest military honours:
Param Vishisht Seva Medal (PVSM)
Ati Vishisht Seva Medal (AVSM)
Sena Medal
Vishisht Seva Medal

Lieutenant General NS Raja Subramani is currently serving as the Military Adviser at the National Security Council Secretariat, a position he has held since September 1, 2025.
Before this, he served as the Vice Chief of the Army Staff from July 1, 2024, to July 31, 2025.

tweets.title:
China Confirms On‑Ground Role In Supporting Pakistan During Operation Sindoor

China has, for the first time, officially admitted that its personnel were present on Pakistani soil during Operation Sindoor, providing direct technical support to the Pakistan Air Force.

This revelation came through China’s state broadcaster CCTV, where engineers from the Aviation Industry Corporation of China (AVIC) described their involvement in supporting Pakistani operations. 

Zhang Heng, an engineer from AVIC’s Chengdu Aircraft Design and Research Institute, recounted his experiences at Pakistani air bases during the conflict. He described the constant roar of fighter jets, the wail of air‑raid sirens, and the extreme heat that reached 50 degrees Celsius by late morning.

The admission marks a significant moment in the India‑Pakistan conflict narrative, as Beijing had previously refrained from acknowledging any on‑ground role in the confrontation.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Day 3 Outcome

Day 3 witnessed intense drone duels and the expansion of the conflict into the maritime domain as India enforced multi-domain pressure on Pakistan.
1. Air Defence Mastery: Indian forces achieved near-total interception of over 600 Pakistani drones. The IACCS system proved highly effective, integrating multiple sensors and platforms to maintain a robust air defence shield along the IB and LoC.
2. Naval Compellence: The forward deployment of the INS Vikrant Carrier Battle Group into the northern Arabian Sea created a powerful deterrent. By positioning strike assets within range of Karachi Port, India imposed serious economic and strategic pressure on Pakistan’s primary maritime lifeline.
3. Strategic Signalling: India demonstrated its ability to operate simultaneously across land, air, and sea domains. The naval posturing added a new layer of compellence, pinning Pakistani naval forces and limiting their options for escalation.

tweets.title:
Vice Admiral Krishna Swaminathan has been appointed as the next Chief of Naval Staff. He is currently serving as the Western Naval Commander in Mumbai and is set to take charge on May 31. His tenure will last until December 31, 2028. He will take over as the head of the Indian Navy after Admiral Dinesh Kumar Tripathi retires.

Commissioned into the Indian Navy on July 1, 1987, Vice Admiral Swaminathan specialises in Communication and Electronic Warfare. Throughout his career, he has held several key positions, including Chief of Personnel for the Navy, Controller of Personnel Services, and Chief of Staff for the Western Naval Command based in Mumbai.

The flag officer is an alumnus of the National Defence Academy in Khadakwasla, the Joint Services Command and Staff College in Shrivenham, United Kingdom, the College of Naval Warfare in Karanja, and the United States Naval War College in Newport, Rhode Island, USA.

tweets.title:
Türkiye and Hungary join forces to develop the Tolga counter-drone UGV for future warfare.
Türkiye and Hungary are moving to field a mobile robotic counter-drone defence system after Turkish defence company MKE signed an MoU with Hungary’s HT Division at SAHA 2026 in Istanbul to integrate the Tolga 20 mm counter-UAS weapon onto unmanned ground vehicles.

 The partnership matters because it combines low-cost drone interception, electronic warfare, and autonomous mobility into a deployable battlefield asset designed to protect manoeuvre forces and logistics nodes from the growing threat posed by FPV and tactical UAV attacks.

The Tolga system combines AESA radar, RF jamming, EO/IR tracking, and fragmentation-based gun systems into a layered architecture built to defeat mass drone attacks without relying on expensive missile interceptors.

In a major leadership transition for India’s maritime forces, the Government of India has appointed Vice Admiral Krishna Swaminathan as the next Chief of the Naval Staff. A specialist in Communication and Electronic Warfare, Vice Admiral Swaminathan brings nearly four decades of distinguished service in the Indian Navy. His remarkable career includes command of frontline warships such as INS Vidyut, INS Vinash, INS Kulish, INS Mysore and the aircraft carrier INS Vikramaditya.

He has also served in several key appointments, including Flag Officer Commanding Western Fleet, Chief of Staff of the Western Naval Command, Vice Chief of the Naval Staff, and most recently as Flag Officer Commanding-in-Chief of the Western Naval Command.

With advanced academic credentials, including a PhD in International Studies and strategic education from institutions in India, the United Kingdom and the United States, Vice Admiral Swaminathan is widely regarded as one of the Navy’s most accomplished operational commanders and strategic thinkers.

He takes over at a critical time when India is expanding its blue-water naval capabilities, strengthening indigenous shipbuilding, modernising submarine and aircraft carrier programmes, and responding to China’s growing naval presence in the Indian Ocean Region. Under his leadership, the Indian Navy is expected to further enhance maritime security, regional partnerships, and India’s role as a leading security provider in the Indo-Pacific.

8th May 2026

- We will be posting daily war updates on the ongoing war in Middle East
- ⁠Follow us for daily Brief.

● Saudi Arabia and Kuwait restored full US access to their military bases and airspace following the launch of Project Freedom.

● New reports reveal that over 75,000 international flights have been canceled from June to September due to soaring fuel costs.

● Iran seized the tanker Ocean Koi, claiming it was disrupting Iranian oil exports.

● UAE MoD announced that on May 8 the UAE air defence systems engaged 2 ballistic missiles and 3 UAV’s launched from Iran, resulting in 3 moderate injuries.

● Iranian FM Araghchi condemned US strikes on sites in Bandar Abbas and Qeshm Island, accusing Washington of choosing “reckless military adventure” over diplomacy.

● US forces disabled two tankers (M/T Sea Star III and M/T Sevda) in the Gulf of Oman to enforce the naval blockade.

● Marco Rubio stated the US will only negotiate with Lebanon’s legitimate government and will not engage with Hezbollah, calling the group an obstacle to a strong Lebanese state.

● The IDF dismantled 8 underground Hamas tunnel routes totaling over 2.5 km in length, including infrastructure in eastern Rafah.

END OF THE BRIEF

7th May 2026

- We will be posting daily war updates on the ongoing war in Middle East
- ⁠Follow us for daily Brief.

● Iran’s Ports and Maritime Organisation declared readiness to provide fuel, provisions, medical assistance, and limited repairs to commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz.

● The US presented a tough proposal demanding Iran dismantle the Fordow, Natanz, and Isfahan facilities, accept a 20-year enrichment moratorium, hand over its uranium stockpiles, allow on-demand inspections, and ban all underground nuclear work.

● The IDF dismantled a ~30-meter underground tunnel in the Labbouneh area and over 200 Hezbollah infrastructure sites in southern Lebanon.

● Iran imposed new regulations requiring ships to submit an application for approval before transiting the Strait of Hormuz.

● Israel and Lebanon are set to hold talks again next Thursday in Washington centered on the cessation of hostilities in southern Lebanon and the disarmament of Lebanese Hezbollah.

● Iranian President Pezeshkian said any negotiation on fully reopening the Strait of Hormuz requires the US to first lift its naval blockade.

tweets.title:
DRDO has successfully conducted the maiden flight trial of the TARA (Tactical Air-to-Surface Retarding Aerodynamic kit) on 7 May 2026 off the coast of Odisha.
The trial involved the TARA 500 variant, which is designed to convert unguided bombs into precision-guided glide weapons with significantly extended range.
This indigenous modular glide kit represents a key advancement in India’s precision munitions capabilities. It enhances the IAF’s deep-strike potential by enabling accurate engagement of targets from safer standoff distances while utilising existing warhead inventories.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Day 2 Outcome:

Day 2 marked a pivotal transition from precision strikes on terrorist infrastructure to high-intensity multi-domain warfare focused on Escalation Dominance and Suppression of Enemy Air Defenses (SEAD). While Pakistan launched a massive retaliatory effort, India utilized indigenous technology and deceptive tactics to establish conventional superiority.

1⃣ Suppression of Pakistani Air Defenses (SEAD): In a sophisticated mission, the Indian Air Force (IAF) successfully neutralized a critical Chinese-supplied HQ-9B surface-to-air missile (SAM) unit in Lahore. This strike, along with the destruction of radar installations in Gujranwala, effectively blinded the Pakistan Air Force’s (PAF) early warning network in the Punjab sector.

2⃣ Success of Deceptive Tactics: India employed "dummy aircraft" to fool Pakistani radar operators. This tactic lured the PAF into activating their defense systems, exposing their positions for subsequent precision strikes by Indian anti-radiation missiles.

3⃣ Total Interception of Drone Swarms: Pakistan attempted to overwhelm Indian defenses by launching between 600 and 1,000 drones, including Turkish-origin ASISGUARD Songar and Chinese Wing Loong II units. The Indian Integrated Counter-UAS Grid, powered by the indigenous Akashteer command and control system and Akash missile batteries, achieved a near-perfect kill rate, neutralising over 600 drones along the western front.

4⃣ Maritime Compellence: The INS Vikrant Carrier Battle Group (CBG), operating in the northern Arabian Sea, maintained a combat air patrol (CAP) that pinned the Pakistan Navy to its coast, effectively enforcing a posture of maritime compellence and preventing any flanking maneuvers from the Makran coast.
